critic_raw: The CNN-LSTM hybrid vs. logistic regression experiment for 30-day hospital readmission has a strong overall design but several methodological concerns. First, the 1D CNN component is applied to temporal visit sequences to 'extract local patterns in visit dynamics'—but the visits are irregularly spaced (clinical visits do not occur at uniform intervals), and 1D CNN with fixed-size convolutional kernels assumes temporal regularity. The design specifies temporal features (days since last admission, rolling windows) but does not address how the CNN handles variable inter-visit intervals in the raw sequence. Second, the logistic regression baseline uses the identical feature set as the CNN-LSTM, including temporal features. This means the comparison directly tests architectural capacity: both models receive the same features, and the logistic regression is expected to underperform primarily because it cannot model nonlinear sequential interactions. This is a cleaner comparison than most, but it may be overclaiming if simpler nonlinear models (e.g., gradient boosted trees with the same features) would achieve comparable performance without the sequential architecture. Third, the validation set spans only the first half of year 4, meaning validation data is from approximately 18 months of the 5-year span—this may not adequately represent the full seasonal and temporal distribution of the test set (second half of year 4 and all of year 5).
all_issues_raised: 1D CNN assumes temporal regularity in visit sequences that are inherently irregular
Gradient-boosted trees with same features not compared—CNN-LSTM advantage over nonlinear baselines unestablished
Validation set covers only 6 months vs. 18-month test set—distributional mismatch possible

round_transcripts.critic: The CNN-LSTM experiment has three concerns: the 1D CNN cannot natively handle irregular inter-visit intervals; simpler nonlinear baselines (GBT) are not compared, leaving the sequential architecture's advantage over general nonlinear modeling unestablished; and the validation set's 6-month window vs. 18-month test window may create distributional mismatch.
round_transcripts.defender: On irregular intervals: the CNN operates on fixed-length sequence representations where inter-visit timing is encoded as a feature within each visit vector (days since last admission). The CNN learns over position-indexed sequences after time normalization; it does not natively understand irregular timing but the timing information is available as a feature, which is standard practice. On GBT comparison: logistic regression is the clinically standard baseline the hypothesis specifies; adding a GBT would be an ablation for architectural attribution but is not required for testing the stated hypothesis. On validation window: 6 months is sufficient for hyperparameter selection; the validation set is not required to match the test distribution—it is used for early stopping and model selection.
round_transcripts.adversary_note: The irregular interval encoding as a feature is a reasonable mitigation but does not allow the CNN to dynamically weight longer-gap visits differently from shorter-gap visits during convolution—this remains a limitation of fixed-kernel 1D CNN for clinical sequences.

round_transcripts.critic: The defender's response is technically correct on all three points as procedural defenses. The irregular interval concern remains a genuine modeling limitation: encoding timing as a feature does not give the 1D CNN the same expressive power as a time-aware attention mechanism (e.g., Transformer with positional encoding) or a continuous-time model (e.g., ODE-RNN). The 6-month validation window concern is procedurally acceptable but the design should verify that validation and test AUC are consistent—a large validation-to-test AUC gap would indicate the validation set is not representative. These are limitations, not invalidating flaws.
round_transcripts.defender: Agreed: the irregular interval handling is a modeling approximation with known limitations. The validation-to-test consistency check is a reasonable diagnostic to include in the analysis. These are scope limitations that should be acknowledged rather than fatal flaws in the experimental design.

all_issues_adjudicated: 1D CNN's handling of irregular visit intervals is a modeling approximation that may limit performance vs. time-aware models
